topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a jarring intrusion in the quiet afternoon. My breath hitched, a small, involuntary catch in my chest. It was a profile picture I hadn't seen in fifteen years—a smirk that used to feel like a physical weight on my shoulders. My fingers hovered over the 'Accept' button, the screen reflecting the pale light of my desk lamp. I took a slow, deliberate breath, letting the tension drain from my jaw. I didn't click anything. Instead, I minimized the message and pulled up an old photo album, the familiar comfort of faded memories washing over me. The air in the room seemed to settle, the frantic buzzing in my mind receding into a low hum. I closed the album, the click of the lid a solid, grounding sound.

[story 2]

I stared at the incoming request, my heart beginning a frantic, uneven rhythm against my ribs. For a moment, I felt a cold knot tighten in my stomach, the kind that precedes a storm. Then, I forced myself to lean back in my chair, stretching my arms above my head until my shoulders protested. I focused on the texture of the worn fabric of my hoodie, the slight give of the cushion beneath me. I reread the username—a name I hadn't thought about in years—and a strange, detached amusement flickered across my features. I typed a short, neutral reply, something entirely devoid of warmth, and then I immediately switched off my phone, plunging the room into a sudden, enveloping darkness.

[story 3]

The sound of the notification was loud in the silence of my apartment. I felt my shoulders immediately tense, a familiar bracing action. I watched the little icon glow, analyzing the potential fallout. But instead of reacting, I walked over to the window and looked out at the city lights blurring beneath the glass. The distance between me and that memory, that person, felt vast and uncrossable. I let my gaze drift, letting the expansive view fill my vision until the sharp edges of anxiety softened. I didn't even look back at the screen; I just stood there, letting the ambient noise of the evening drown out the phantom echo of that old conflict.
